summary: I was born in Abilene, Texas on April 11, 1922, and lived in Texas until I finished high school. My early years there were marked by the onset of my blindness after an injury at age eight. Abilene was my home during those formative years before my family moved to California.

entity: Texas School for the Blind and Visually Impaired
summary: After my blindness developed, I was enrolled in the Texas School for the Blind, which I found to be a very poor institution. I remember being housed with thirty other boys in a large loft with no privacy, and the school only offered three vocational paths: piano tuning, chair caning, or broom-making. I was determined to leave because I didn’t want to be limited to those options, and I eventually ran away from the school.

When I ran away from the Texas School for the Blind, I told people I had become ill while visiting relatives in Austin, Texas. This story helped me hitchhike home safely because people believed I was stranded due to an expired railway ticket. Austin was part of the cover story I used during my escape.

After moving to California, my family settled in Bakersfield where I enrolled in junior college for one year. This was part of my educational journey before we moved again to Santa Rosa. Bakersfield marked the beginning of my higher education in California.

Following Bakersfield, my family moved to Santa Rosa, California, where I attended Santa Rosa Junior College for one year. I was proud to be the valedictorian there, which showed my academic dedication despite my blindness. Santa Rosa was an important step in my educational path before attending the University of California, Berkeley.

At Santa Rosa Junior College, I completed one year of study and was honored as the valedictorian of my class. This achievement was significant for me as a blind student and helped prepare me for transfer to a university. It was a key part of my academic development in California.

I attended the University of California, Berkeley, where I pursued a general curriculum since I couldn’t find a specific major that held my interest. Although I had one bad semester with a drop to a B average, I finished with an overall GPA around 2.6 or 2.7. I also completed a year of law school there before leaving to work due to financial constraints.

In the spring of 1947, I began working for the California State Department of Education as a rehabilitation specialist for the blind. I stayed with the Bureau of Vocational Rehabilitation until 1951, when I was appointed to head the orientation program. I have been with the department ever since, dedicating my career to supporting blind individuals.

My work with the Bureau of Vocational Rehabilitation began in 1947 under the California State Department of Education. I served as a rehabilitation specialist for the blind until 1951, when I was promoted to lead the orientation program. This role allowed me to contribute directly to vocational support and training for blind individuals.

While I mentioned the California Council of the Blind as part of my involvement with blind organizations, we have yet to discuss its specific role. I have been connected with it alongside the National Federation of the Blind, contributing to advocacy and support for blind people in California. These organizations have been important in addressing public prejudice and advancing blind individuals' rights.
cues: connected with National Federation of the Blind

summary: I have been involved with the National Federation of the Blind, which works alongside the California Council of the Blind. These organizations have played a key role in fighting public prejudice and promoting opportunities for blind people. My work and experiences have often intersected with their efforts to improve the lives of the blind community.
